title: The Bridge
subtitle: Prophetic Evangelism
tagline: Living on mission every day of your Christian life
description: Move from awkward to authentic in sharing your faith. Learn to craft your testimony, ask transformative questions, hear God for others, and step into your calling as an ambassador of Christ.

vocabulary.definition: Greek word for gospel; a message being proclaimed, preached, or testified. The root of our word 'evangelism.'
From Old English 'god spel' meaning 'good story.' The message of salvation through Jesus Christ.
Your personal account of what God has done in your life. The lifeline of the church.
To teach Christian principles through questions and answers. Where we get the word 'catechism.'
Hebrew word meaning to hear, listen, and obey. Listening with the intention of being propelled into action.
Greek for 'doer.' A producer, performer, or one who lives out what they hear. Root of our word 'poet.'
Prophecy that predicts a future event to warn or prepare people for what's coming.
Prophecy that makes public God's plans so they can be carried out by the hearer.
Greek for compassion. To be moved deeply in your inward parts, like gut-wrenching love.
Greek for 'thrust out' or 'send violently.' The same word used when Jesus casts out demons.
Greek for authority. The power to act on behalf of one with great power.
Greek for 'earnestly desire.' To be zealous or jealous for something.

sessionOverviews.title: Euangelion
sessionOverviews.synopsis: Discover what evangelism really means and why your personal testimony is the most powerful tool you have. Learn to craft your 'good story' using the Conflict, Turning Point, and Result framework.

sessionOverviews.title: The Best Ever
sessionOverviews.synopsis: Passion is the fuel for evangelism. Explore why the Gospel without a transformed heart is just news, and learn from a real testimony of God at work in unexpected places.

sessionOverviews.title: The Art of Asking Questions
sessionOverviews.synopsis: Jesus asked 173 questions. Learn the three types of questions that lead people from surface conversation to transformative encounter with God.

sessionOverviews.title: Prophesy or Profi-lie?
sessionOverviews.synopsis: Prophecy is not just for 'prophets.' Discover how to hear God for others and deliver His heart with accuracy and love through various prophetic vehicles.

sessionOverviews.title: Compelled By Love
sessionOverviews.synopsis: The love of Christ compels us. Learn the three Greek words that unlock the secret to being sent with power: splagna, ekballo, and exousia.

sessionOverviews.title: Training Day
sessionOverviews.synopsis: Time to put it all into practice. Learn practical strategies for surveying, door knocking, and everyday conversations that lead to eternal impact.

sessions.title: Euangelion
sessions.synopsis: Discover what evangelism really means and why your personal testimony is the most powerful tool you have. Learn to craft your 'good story' using the Conflict, Turning Point, and Result framework.
sessions.verse: We should be more concerned with reaching the lost than pampering the saved.
warmUp: Evangelism. What comes to mind? What feeling does this word give you?
What kind of person do you imagine would be a good evangelist?

supportingScriptures.text: Go, therefore, and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, teaching them to follow all that I commanded you; and behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age.
But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be My witnesses both in Jerusalem and in all Judea, and Samaria, and as far as the remotest part of the earth.
And He said to them, 'Go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation.'

content.text: How, then, can they call on the one they have not believed in? And how can they believe in the one of whom they have not heard? And how can they hear without someone preaching to them? And how can anyone preach unless they are sent? As it is written: 'How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news!'
Evangelism is simply the act of sharing your faith in something with another.

When I was far from God and promoting nightclubs, I was simply evangelizing for the enemy and his kingdom. But now I am dedicated to telling people about the God who heals, saves and delivers all who receive His love. If you are in this class you too have been touched by this God and your heart burns for others to experience the same thing.

Many people are scared of sharing their faith because they think they have to know the Bible front to back and have all the right answers. I'm here to tell you that all you need to share your faith in Jesus is a passion for people. Love will compel you to share. Jesus is passionate about people even when they reject Him. Even atheists and criminals, who you may find it easy to write off. Yes, Jesus is even passionate about them and he wants to use you to love them into His kingdom.
But as for you, use self-restraint in all things, endure hardship, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.

questions: What has held you back from sharing your faith in the past?
Do you believe you need to have all the answers before you can evangelize?

lessons.title: The History of The Good News

content.text: Let's take some time and uncover the origin of two misunderstood words that mean the same thing: evangelism and the gospel.

The Old English word gospel breaks down to god meaning 'good' and spel meaning 'story'. From this we get the contemporary term 'good news'. In the New Testament Bible the Greek word for gospel is {{euangelion|Greek word for gospel; a message being proclaimed, preached, or testified}} and is most often used to mean 'a message' being proclaimed, preached or testified. When the writers of the four Gospels were writing they were simply proclaiming their version of God's story.

This is the disciple who is testifying to these things and wrote these things, and we know that his testimony is true. And there are also many other things which Jesus did, which if they were written in detail, I suppose that even the world itself would not contain the books that would be written.

As the body of Christ our gospel 'God story' continues to be recorded, remembered and proclaimed. The revelation of Christ's power is still being told through us who experience and participate in his goodness. Testimonies are the lifeline of the church, not only for our spiritual fire, but for those who hear, receive and share the inheritance of these works. Without our testimony, who will tell of His mighty right arm? Who will proclaim the miracles of God?

The Spirit of the Lord God is upon me, because the Lord has anointed me to bring good news to the afflicted; He has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to captives and freedom to prisoners.

For what I received I passed on to you as of first importance: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, and then to the Twelve.

questions: Who was the first person to share the gospel with you?

content.text: You have 280 characters to share the Good News of Jesus Christ, what do you write?

Here's an example of 280 characters:

God loves us, but sin separates us. Jesus, God's Son, died for our sins and rose again, offering forgiveness and eternal life to those who believe and follow Him.

content.prompt: Write your 280-character gospel message. What would you say if you only had a tweet to share the Good News?

content.text: Communicating your story clearly and passionately is key to the impact it will have on the listener's heart. Jesus is a master communicator and the Bible is a well organized love story of redemption, triumph and empowerment. If we rely on Him through this testimony process we will be amazed with the outcome.
Any good story has three main parts: Conflict, Turning Point, and Result.

For example, if I were to sum up the Bible using these three parts, I would say: 'God created people to be like Him. However, they got sick with sin so He sent His own Son, Jesus, to heal us through His death and resurrection. Now those who receive this healing have the ability to be holy like God.' Conflict, Turning Point and Result.

For me, my one paragraph testimony would go like this:

'I was a greedy, alcoholic womanizer searching for love in all the wrong places. Through this search I destroyed my marriage and hurt lots of people. I found myself in a really dark place where I asked "God if you are real, show me." God revealed Himself to me in December of 2009 and I encountered the love of God for the first time. I was never the same after this moment. Now I am healed, my marriage is healthy and I help lead people to Jesus everyday of my life.'

This is a great starting place. Normally you will have a person's full attention for 60 seconds which allows you to fit more passion and details that tell a full and compelling story. It is much easier to add details later once you have established the foundation. How can you communicate your story in as few words as possible while providing enough detail to make it unique from the next person?

content.prompt: Write your testimony in under 100 words using the Conflict, Turning Point, and Result framework. This is the version you might share if you had someone's attention for 20 seconds or less.
Your assignment this week is to bring your testimony typed and printed to share with the group.
content.action: Write your 100-word testimony. Be concise, sincere, and full of passion. Bring it printed next session.

sessions.title: The Best Ever
sessions.synopsis: Passion is the fuel for evangelism. Explore why the Gospel without a transformed heart is just news, and learn from a real testimony of God at work in unexpected places.
sessions.verse: There is none like You, O Lord; You are great, and great is Your name in might.
warmUp: What's the best movie you have ever seen? And why?

supportingScriptures.text: For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
Therefore, we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God were making an appeal through us; we beg you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God.
Always be prepared to give an answer to everyone who asks you to give the reason for the hope that you have. But do this with gentleness and respect.

content.text: Even after all these years I still get excited over the movie Braveheart. The tragedy and triumph, the act of laying yourself down for something you believe in so much that life itself is worth losing. When I find someone who has not seen Braveheart, I make it my mission to make it their next movie night. 'No, you don't understand. It's the best movie ever. You have to see this. Here's five bucks for your iTunes rental. I'll be over at 7 with popcorn and half of my face painted blue.'
For when I preach the gospel, I cannot boast, since I am compelled to preach. Woe to me if I do not preach the gospel!
The Gospel of Jesus Christ without the passion of a transformed heart is just a news story read from a teleprompter.
We are therefore Christ's ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us. We implore you on Christ's behalf: Be reconciled to God.

content.title: Do you really believe Jesus is the best thing you have ever experienced?

content.text: If yes, there is nothing anyone could do to stop you from telling people about Him and the amazing things He has done in your life. Anyone with a heart on fire for God will share the gospel. They can't hold back the passion of the best story ever.

When I first met Jesus I was the shotgun Christian who recklessly sprayed 'my' gospel on every person around me. I did a better job of causing people to avoid me than attracting people to Jesus. I was passionate, on fire and Jesus was the best thing I had ever experienced.

Over time God has refined my ability to share my faith. I went from a shotgun to a sniper. The passion to share the gospel has not changed but the restraint of when and how I share has. I am compelled to share the best story my heart has ever known. But God has refined the package in which it is delivered.

content.prompt: Do you believe Jesus is the best thing you have ever experienced? If your answer is hesitant, what is holding you back from that level of conviction?

content.text: If you declare with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved. For it is with your heart that you believe and are justified, and it is with your mouth that you profess your faith and are saved.

What if I told you that the sinner's prayer technique of salvation was not created until the 1740's and not made popular until the 1940's by the Billy Graham crusades. The sinner's prayer was never used by the early church to proclaim the Gospel of Jesus Christ. In fact we can't find any scriptures that anyone ever prayed a specific prayer to receive Jesus.
Repent. Believe. Be Baptized.

What we do see is someone comes to a town and begins to tell people about Jesus and the amazing testimonies that surrounded His life. Whoever is sharing, does so in a way that relates to the culture of that place. Then people get healed, demons get cast out and people are prophesied to as a sign of God's power. Then people respond. They either say 'Yes, I want that.' or 'No, you're crazy and I'm offended by what you bring.' Those who say yes are {{baptized|The act of being immersed under water or in the Holy Spirit as a declaration of faith}} in water and in the Holy Spirit. They are empowered to do the same in their community.

In western culture, the modern church movement has taken the simple gospel of the Kingdom and replaced it with concerts, emotional decisions and trips down the roman road of depravity. 'Everyone bow your heads so no one will be looking at those who say the sinner's prayer which we will all say together every week so no one will feel embarrassed.'

Now I'm not saying we should not give people invitations to say yes to the gift of salvation. I am saying that I'd like to see the people of God move in power. I want to see the saints gather, celebrate and train each other for the works of ministry. The church body then takes this training and works with Holy Spirit to administer the miracle of salvation to the people around them. That is evangelism.

questions: When you think about the act of sharing 'your good story', do you feel excitement, passion, nervousness or maybe a feeling of blah? 'That's just not my thing'?
Why do you feel this way?

content.text: One of the coolest testimonies of God using me to reach someone was at a water park in Las Vegas called Wet and Wild. As I was walking up to the entrance with my family, I saw this guy shirtless with tattoos all over, scraggly hair and piercings. He had one tattoo in particular that really stood out. It was a giant deer head with antlers that went up his shoulders and in the middle was an inverted cross. This was the kind of guy if my daughter ever brought to dinner I might lose my salvation.

As we found seats I saw this guy again in the wave pool and this time God spoke to my heart. He said 'Wouldn't it be cool if you baptized that guy today?' I replied, 'Yeah that would be cool but how the heck could that happen?'

As I'm floating down the lazy river, the guy appears again and this time he looks really suspicious. He is sitting at different chairs and looking around at people's stuff. It wasn't like I could watch him for very long as the river turned a corner but I knew something wasn't right about this situation.

Walking back to our seats I see him again sitting at some chairs looking through a bag. He then throws a towel over the bag and begins to walk towards the exit. I immediately ran over to him and said 'Hey, where are you going with that bag?!' Startled, he says 'I I I I'm just waiting for my party.'

There we were, full stare down, as a tumbleweed rolled by. Whawhawhawooow Wowwowwow. The western music begins. I looked at him dead in His eyes and said 'look man, I know what you are doing and you don't need to lie to me, just put the bag back and we won't have any problems.' Finally he realizes I'm not moving and walks over to return the bag.

As soon as he puts it down and walks away, the owner returns and a security guard strolls by. I have the perfect opportunity to turn this guy in, to put him under the law and have justice served. That's when I hear God speak to my heart again and he says 'Tell him your story and show him the grace I gave to you.'

We ended up sitting down at one of the lunch tables where I shared my testimony of how God transformed my life. I said 'Look man, earlier, God told me I was supposed to baptize you today. I want to pray with you and if you feel good about being baptized we can do that.' I prayed that the peace of God would come over him and that his relationship with God would become real just as it had for me. I said, 'John, would you like to take that step?' and he said, 'yes.'

My heart jumped inside my chest as I witnessed a broken man become receptive to God's love. After explaining what baptism meant we walked over to the lazy river, gathered some witnesses and baptized this tattooed thief right there on the spot.

I still remember what he said after he came up from the water. 'My mom's not going to believe this actually happened.' But it did and it was real. He even said he felt electricity on his neck and back. It made me think of when Jesus was baptized and the Holy Spirit rested on Him like a dove. Maybe John was receiving the same gift. I gave John my contact, $20 and invited him to church the next morning but I never saw him again. Maybe one day he will call and tell me how God has transformed his life. In Christ I pray.

questions: What did you notice about the testimony?
What is your response to this good story?

content.text: You are our letter, written in our hearts, known and read by all people, revealing yourselves, that you are a letter of Christ, delivered by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts.
You are a living letter. Your life is being read by everyone around you. The testimony you carry is not just words on a page but a life transformed by the Spirit of the living God.

content.prompt: Expand your 100-word testimony to 400-700 words. What details are necessary to the story? How can you add more passion and specifics that make your story unique?
Your assignment for this week is two-fold:
content.action: 1. Complete your 400-700 word testimony with full details and passion. 2. Ask God for a fresh testimony this coming week that you can bring back to class. Use the Conflict, Turning Point, Result framework and write it down.

sessions.title: The Art of Asking Questions
sessions.synopsis: Jesus asked 173 questions. Learn the three types of questions that lead people from surface conversation to transformative encounter with God.
sessions.verse: People don't care how much you know till they know how much you care.
warmUp: What are some good evangelistic questions?

supportingScriptures.text: When Jesus came to the region of Caesarea Philippi, he asked his disciples, 'Who do people say the Son of Man is?' They replied, 'Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others, Jeremiah or one of the prophets.' 'But what about you?' he asked. 'Who do you say I am?' Simon Peter answered, 'You are the Messiah, the Son of the living God.'
When Jesus saw him lying there and learned that he had been in this condition for a long time, he asked him, 'Do you want to get well?'
Jesus answered her, 'If you knew the gift of God and who it is that asks you for a drink, you would have asked him and he would have given you living water.'

content.text: When Jesus came to the region of Caesarea Philippi, he asked his disciples, 'Who do people say the Son of Man is?' They replied, 'Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others, Jeremiah or one of the prophets.' 'But what about you?' he asked. 'Who do you say I am?' Simon Peter answered, 'You are the Messiah, the Son of the living God.'

All throughout Jesus's life we see Him asking specific questions in order to pull the truth out of those listening. 173 direct questions to be exact. He came to tear down the very isolation we put ourselves in. We set ourselves against God in our own minds and He questioned our delusion.

This act of questioning varied from person to person in whatever misunderstanding they were in. To the religious leader; it was one question, to the disciple; another and to the woman at the well; another.

To {{catechize|To teach Christian principles through questions and answers}} means to instruct Christian principles using questions and answers. This is where we get the term 'Catechism'. Studies suggest that people retain more information through questions and answers than through lecture style teaching. People don't want to be told the answers, they want to discover the answers. Uncovering information is part of the life adventure we were created for.

Maybe your fear around evangelism is because you feel the need to tell people the right information about Jesus when our true purpose is to ask people the right questions. Who are you? What have you been through? Where are you going? Who is Jesus to you? Are we spewing information on people when we should be inviting people into an authentic relationship? No one likes to be sold or told what to do. But if I lead you to the goodness of God through questions that bring about your own realization. That's relational evangelism.
When Jesus saw him lying there and learned that he had been in this condition for a long time, he asked him, 'Do you want to get well?'

content.prompt: Have you been trying to tell people about Jesus when you should be asking questions that lead them to discover Him for themselves?

content.text: The questions we ask are important, but so is the way we ask them. Many people feel condemned in their sin when they are questioned about Jesus. Evangelism can feel more like an interrogation than an invitation into a relationship. Our questioning needs to be Holy Spirit led. We need wisdom and inside information to be invited into someone's heart where the Gospel can take root.

Basic questions that help you gather small pieces of information and build rapport. Are you from here? What do you do for a living? What do you do for fun? I like to ask God questions about them. Or ask Him for questions I should ask. What does God want me to know about this person that I do not know now?

content.prompt: List some surface level questions you could use to start conversations:

content.text: These questions dig deeper into their thought process and world view. They help open their hearts for deeper conversation. What is your biggest fear? What is your dream job? Were you raised with faith?

content.prompt: List some personal questions that dig deeper:

content.text: These questions force people out of their current reality and into the realm of possibilities. These questions cause people to think about things they have never pondered before. Questions like these will expose {{strongholds|Mental or spiritual fortresses that hold people captive to lies and prevent freedom}}, orphan thinking and their deep desire to be known by God.

content.prompt: List some transformative questions that could lead to spiritual breakthrough:

questions: Which type of question do you find easiest to ask?
Which type feels most uncomfortable? Why?

content.text: When we move past the surface and personal level questions, there are three transformative questions I like to use. These tools lead people to think about God and themselves with a fresh perspective. You may recognize these as 'the three identities' from ID3. Who is God? Who are you? How does God see you? You can phrase them in many different ways but the concept is the same.
I may say 'When you think about your creator what's the first thing that comes into your mind?' or 'When you look in the mirror what do you see? How would you describe yourself?'

Most people can answer the first two questions because everyone has an opinion about who they think God is and who they think they are. Even atheists have a perception of God. They may not call it God but they still have an opinion. The first two, most people are willing and able to answer. But the third question 'When your creator looks at you what do they see?' This question requires more than just an opinion; it requires them to have an experience which most people have not had. This gives you an opportunity.
You can a) Lead them to ask God on the spot if you feel they would ask with sincerity. Or b) Ask God how He sees them and deliver the message as a prophetic word.

Time to practice with friends, family members, co-workers and complete strangers. Grab someone in the group and take turns asking surface level questions. See how quickly you can get past the awkwardness and into personal questions. Maybe the answers they give you lead to more questions that increase in intimacy until they lead to a place of transformation.

There is a moment in a conversation that God wants to release His power and He wants to use you to do it. You must discern when this moment is by listening to Holy Spirit. We don't want to tell people about Jesus. We want to introduce them directly to Him. Let the Lord use you now even in this time of practice.

content.prompt: Practice sparking up conversations with people using all of the different question types.
content.action: See how long it takes to get to a transformative place in each conversation. Take notice of what walls you run into. Look for opportunities to share your good story. After a number of questions they may start asking you questions and you will have the opportunity to share what Jesus has done.

content.text: Study these questions Jesus asked throughout His ministry. Notice how each question was designed to draw out truth and lead people to transformation.

1. Why do you worry about clothes? (Matthew 6:28)
2. Why are you so afraid? (Matthew 8:26)
3. Which is easier: to say, 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say, 'Get up and walk'? (Matthew 9:5)
4. How can the guests of the bridegroom mourn while he is with them? (Matthew 9:15)
5. Do you believe that I am able to do this? (Matthew 9:28)
6. What did you go out into the desert to see? (Matthew 11:7)
7. If any of you has a sheep and it falls into a pit on the Sabbath, will you not take hold of it and lift it out? (Matthew 12:11)
8. How can anyone enter a strong man's house and carry off his possessions unless he first ties up the strong man? (Matthew 12:29)
9. Who is my mother, and who are my brothers? (Matthew 12:48)
10. Do you still not understand? (Matthew 16:9)

11. Who do people say the Son of Man is? (Matthew 16:13)
12. Can you drink the cup I am going to drink? (Matthew 20:22)
13. What do you want me to do for you? (Matthew 20:32)
14. Which is greater: the gold, or the temple that makes the gold sacred? (Matthew 23:17-19)
15. How will you escape being condemned to hell? (Matthew 23:33)
16. Why are you bothering this woman? (Matthew 26:10)
17. Could you men not keep watch with me for one hour? (Matthew 26:40)
18. Do you think I cannot call on my Father, and he will at once put at my disposal more than twelve legions of angels? (Matthew 26:53)
19. But how then would the Scriptures be fulfilled that say it must happen in this way? (Matthew 26:54)
20. Am I leading a rebellion, that you have come out with swords and clubs to capture me? (Matthew 26:55)

21. What is your name? (Mark 5:9)
22. Why then is it written that the Son of Man must suffer much and be rejected? (Mark 9:12)
23. Why do you call me good? (Mark 10:18)
24. Why are you thinking these things in your hearts? (Luke 5:22)
25. Why do you call me, 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say? (Luke 6:46)
26. Where is your faith? (Luke 8:25)
27. Who touched me? (Luke 8:45)
28. What is written in the Law? How do you read it? (Luke 10:26)
29. Which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers? (Luke 10:36)
30. Did not the one who made the outside make the inside also? (Luke 11:40)

31. Who appointed me a judge or an arbiter between you? (Luke 12:14-15)
32. Why don't you judge for yourselves what is right? (Luke 12:57)
33. Salt is good, but if it loses its saltiness, how can it be made salty again? (Luke 14:34)
34. And will not God bring about justice for his chosen ones, who cry out to him day and night? (Luke 18:7)
35. What are you discussing together as you walk along? (Luke 24:17)
36. Why are you troubled, and why do doubts rise in your minds? (Luke 24:38)
37. You are Israel's teacher, and do you not understand these things? (John 3:10)
38. I have spoken to you of earthly things and you do not believe; how then will you believe if I speak of heavenly things? (John 3:12)
39. Do you want to get well? (John 5:6)
40. If you do not believe Moses' writings how will you believe me? (John 5:47)

41. Does this offend you? (John 6:61)
42. Why are you angry with me for healing the whole man on the Sabbath? (John 7:23)
43. Where are they? Has no one condemned you? (John 8:10)
44. Why is my language not clear to you? (John 8:43)
45. Can any of you prove me guilty of sin? (John 8:46)
46. Do you believe this? (John 11:26)
47. Do you understand what I have done for you? (John 13:12)
48. Why are you crying? Who is it you are looking for? (John 20:15)
49. Friends, haven't you any fish? (John 21:5)
50. Do you love me? (John 21:17)

content.prompt: Which of these questions stands out to you most? Why? How could you use a similar question in your own conversations?

sessions.title: Prophesy or Profi-lie?
sessions.synopsis: Prophecy is not just for 'prophets.' Discover how to hear God for others and deliver His heart with accuracy and love through various prophetic vehicles.
sessions.verse: Then I fell at his feet to worship him. But he said to me, 'Do not do that; I am a fellow servant of yours and your brothers and sisters who hold the testimony of Jesus; worship God! For the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy.'
warmUp: What is prophecy? How does this word make you feel?

supportingScriptures.text: Pursue love, yet earnestly desire spiritual gifts, but especially that you may prophesy.
But the one who prophesies speaks to people for edification, exhortation, and consolation.
For we know in part and prophesy in part; but when the perfect comes, the partial will be done away with.

content.text: Pursue love, yet earnestly desire spiritual gifts, but especially that you may prophesy.

Prophecy is one of those spiritual terms that may be more of a mystery than a reality in your life. Most likely because some of the church has shied away from teaching prophecy as a new covenant practice. The gift and calling of prophecy is just as active under the new covenant as it was under the old. Though, the context and purpose has changed due to the atonement of Christ.
Prophecy is the supernatural act of receiving information from the Holy Spirit and proclaiming that message at the right time to the intended listener.

Prophecy can be delivered in a variety of communication vehicles. All of them can be useful to minister to the good news to the people you meet. Here are a few: {{foretelling|Prophecy that predicts a future event to warn or prepare people}}, {{forthtelling|Prophecy that makes public God's plans so they can be carried out}}, dreams, visions and interpretation. In this session, you will exercise your prophetic gift in a safe place. Our goal is to give you opportunities to take a step that will change the way you look at evangelism forever. Will you take this gift of prophecy with you into the world?

1 Corinthians 14:1 says 'Pursue love: yet earnestly desire spiritual gifts...' Earnestly desire is the Greek word {{zeloo|Greek for earnestly desire; to be zealous or jealous for something}}. It could be translated as 'be zealous for' or 'feign for'. It literally means to be jealous. In other words, let love be your highest goal and in the process be jealous to possess spiritual gifts, especially the gift of prophecy.

I want you to be rooted and grounded in the love of God and salivate over the idea of hearing God and being his mouthpiece to broken people in desperate need of His love. In fact your mouth should be watering right now at the mention of spiritual gifts.

Anyone who speaks in other languages builds up only themselves. But the person who prophesies builds up the church. I would like all of you to speak in other languages. But I would rather have you prophesy. The person who prophesies is more helpful than those who speak in other languages. But that is not the case if someone explains what was said in the other languages. Then the whole church can be built up.

content.prompt: Does your mouth 'water' at the idea of spiritual gifts? Or does this topic make you nervous? Why?

content.text: Under the old covenant men were not indwelt by the Holy Spirit. Therefore communication with God to His people was done through specific men and women. The standard of accuracy placed on old covenant prophets was life or death. (Deuteronomy 18:19-20) We have many examples of God's prophets passing on words of judgment to disobedient children. We also see these same prophets declaring the grace and mercy of God to come through the Messiah to be. They rebuke kings and nations. They exalt the salvation ahead.

Old covenant prophets proclaimed God's redemptive plan of Jesus through over 400 prophecies and 1000's of prophetic details fulfilled by Christ. It's as if the first 44 books of the Bible provide a forensic fingerprint of the Messiah that can only match one man's life. From Joseph's life alone, we have over a hundred details that speak to what Jesus would later do.

Under the New Covenant, believers have been filled with the Spirit of God with an open communication line. We no longer need prophets to listen and speak with God on our behalf. We all have direct access. Some of us have matured above others in our ability to listen and discern what we hear. But it is available to all who have said yes.

Even though we no longer need prophets to talk to God for us, New covenant prophecy is extremely valuable for building up the body. Prophecy can be used to warn, confirm, encourage, and bring comfort. It's astounding how much you are willing to go through when the finish line has been declared over you. There may be times you question life through trials, until the Lord sends someone to remind you of your destiny. Even though He could speak to you directly, He gives the words to someone else. He designed us to be in community and loves it when we co-labor as a body.
But the one who prophesies speaks to people for edification, exhortation, and consolation.

questions: Tell us a time God used another believer to communicate with you.
Was it a good experience or bad? What made the difference?

content.text: 'Call to Me and I will answer you, and I will tell you great and mighty things, which you do not know.'

One of the most frequent questions in Christianity is 'How do I hear God's voice and how do I know it's God?' This innate desire to communicate with God shows that you were designed for relationships. Without it you feel like something is missing. And like learning a new language or culture, you must learn the voice of your Shepherd. His sheep know His voice and they obey Him.

The Hebrew word {{shema|Hebrew word meaning to hear, listen, and obey; listening with intention of action}} (shä·mah') means to hear, listen, and obey. Many times we think of the word listen as a passive act. However this word shema implies a much deeper revelation of hearing, listening and then putting what you hear into action. Shema is listening with the intention of being propelled into motion. Much like a dancer who waits for the music to begin so they might begin their movement at the right time.
This concept of listening and putting into action is also found in the New Testament book of James. He writes...

But be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves. For if anyone is a hearer of the word and not a doer, he is like a man observing his natural face in a mirror; for he observes himself, goes away, and immediately forgets what kind of man he was. But he who looks into the perfect law of liberty and continues in it, and is not a forgetful hearer but a doer of the work, this one will be blessed in what he does.

The word 'doer' in this scripture is the Greek word {{poeta|Greek for doer; a producer, performer, one who lives out what they hear}}. This is the same word we get poet from. It means to be a producer, performer, person who lives out. It is clear that when God speaks He is not just looking for someone with ears but someone who will hear and put into action. This could mean you 'shema' a word from the Lord and deliver it as a poet would from a stage. Don't be deceived and forget the image you were created in.

content.prompt: When you hear God speak, do you act on it? Or do you 'look in the mirror and walk away, forgetting what you saw'?

content.text: There are different ways God speaks to me about what people need prayer for. Sometimes He speaks through pain in certain areas of my body to show me the ailment in the person I am supposed to release healing over. Other times, I get an impression or an urgency to pray for someone but I don't know why until I open my mouth by faith and begin to release what God wants to say.
You can receive information from God for: yourself, peers, church, rulers, land, and cultural influence.
Foretelling - to predict a future event that will happen. This can be to warn or prepare people for what's coming.
Forthtelling - to make public the plans of God so they can be carried out by the hearer. If this plan was not made public it may not have happened.
Dreams - a vision from God during a sleep-state that can provide powerful imagery through metaphors and symbols. Not all dreams are prophetic. Some dreams are just dreams. Some dreams are demonic.
Visions - an open visual from God in the natural realm. This can be a powerful way to see things in the spirit that you cannot see with the natural eye.
Interpretation - hearing a dream or vision, discerning the spirit behind the dream and putting words of clarity to the objects and events that were seen.

Impressions - receiving information through the TV screen of your mind. This could come through images, scenes, words, thoughts, feelings etc. For the person who is experiencing impressions there is an element of faith that can be felt.

Signs and Symbols - God confirming things in your life through physical cues, landmarks, connections. Some people would call these coincidences but we believe God is extremely intentional how He speaks with us. I like to call these appearances 'God's bread crumbs'.

Art and Creativity - The ability to put a message from God into a painting or poem. I have experienced this many times through songwriting and other forms of visual art. I may not see the full prophetic value until years later.

Scripture - God will give you exact scriptures at exact moments to read or give to someone. This will be the exact scripture they need to hear at that moment. We love this prophetic gift because it points people back to the word.

questions: Which of these prophetic types have you experienced?
Which would you like to grow in?

content.text: One of the ways we practice the prophetic in a safe place is called a prophetic round table. I find that it is best in a group of 3-5 people. You all sit around a table and take 2-3 mins asking God 'Who is this person to my left?' 'What do you see over them?' 'What have you called them to?' Questions like these are great because they reveal the heart of God for someone in real-time. Plus you each get to take a risk sharing what you heard from God.

It is best to write down everything you saw, heard, felt and experienced as you were seeking God for them. Each of you take turns sharing and giving feedback until the round table is complete. We encourage you to use your voice recorder on your phone and keep a copy of the word that was given. You never know what details will touch that person's heart, so make sure you share everything you wrote down. The receiver is responsible to take what was spoken back to God and test the word. But this is the process.

Sometimes we can see things that are sinful or broken in someone's life. It is important that we do not deliver a word in a way that will cause shame or condemnation. Be sensitive to the nature of what you saw and ask God for wisdom on how to share. Maybe you can pray the opposite of what you saw and push that person out of darkness. Rather than call out their sin, call out their identity in Christ in this area.

Please don't be discouraged if you are challenged to hear or see anything for the person you are praying for. There could be some hindrances in your ability to hear God clearly. We have an awesome mini course called Hindered Hearing that can help you be set free from things that could be holding you back.
Get into groups of 3-5 and spend some time asking God about the person to your left. Have fun!

content.prompt: Write down everything you saw, heard, felt, and experienced as you sought God for the person to your left:
Take 2-3 minutes to ask God: 'Who is this person? What do you see over them? What have you called them to?'

sessions.title: Compelled By Love
sessions.synopsis: The love of Christ compels us. Learn the three Greek words that unlock the secret to being sent with power: splagna, ekballo, and exousia.
sessions.verse: For the love of Messiah compels us, since we have concluded that One died for all; as a result all died. And He died for all so that those who live might no longer live for themselves, but for the One who died for them and was raised.
warmUp: Have you ever felt compelled to share the love of Jesus with someone?
How would you describe that feeling?

supportingScriptures.text: When he saw the crowds, he had compassion on them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd. Then he said to his disciples, 'The harvest is plentiful but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into his harvest field.'
Jesus went through all the towns and villages, teaching in their synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom and healing every disease and sickness.
And Jesus said to him, 'Today salvation has come to this house, because he, too, is a son of Abraham. For the Son of Man has come to seek and to save that which was lost.'

content.text: One misconception of today's faith culture is that we shouldn't offend people with the Gospel. This may sound acceptable in light of cancel-culture but it's just not biblical. All throughout the book of Acts we read one occurrence of disturbance after another. Nearly every city Paul traveled to meant trouble. Not only were people offended by the message of Christ crucified but they were hostile towards the messengers. Their entire way of life was threatened by the Kingdom of Heaven being instituted on earth.

Does this mean we should stand on a street corner and tell people they are going to hell? This is not my preferred method but if the Lord leads you to do this; who am I to tell you different? I would rather have honest conversations with people that leave room for the power of God to be displayed. But here's the thing. When miracles, signs and wonders begin to manifest, the crowd will start to gather. And persecution is not far away.

They followed his advice; and after calling the apostles in, they flogged them and ordered them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and then released them. So they went on their way from the presence of the Council, rejoicing that they had been considered worthy to suffer shame for His name. And every day, in the temple and from house to house, they did not stop teaching and preaching the good news of Jesus as the Christ.

content.prompt: Have you ever held back from sharing the gospel because you were afraid of offending someone? What happened?

content.text: People function out of two zones, comfort and conflict. Some people will do whatever it takes to avoid the conflict zone while others love to live and swim in dangerous waters where things get real. Most people who avoid conflict do so because of unhealthy confrontations they've experienced before. Maybe you've experienced severe aggression or verbal rejection that causes you to flinch anytime the conflict zone is approached. The caution flags of self preservation start to wave violently. Abort! Abort! And so we prematurely halt the conversation and retreat back to the comfort zone.

How do you break past fear and cross over from comfort into conflict without scaring someone away? First of all, the conviction of love must be so strong it compels you to step into risk. This is a love that says 'I know this may be uncomfortable but I am willing to fight for you and the freedom Jesus provides.' We can be sure that loving others recklessly will cost us something. Therefore we must know the value of this love for ourselves before we can offer it to others.

The woman said to Him, 'Sir, give me this water so that I will not be thirsty, nor come all the way here to draw water.' He said to her, 'Go, call your husband and come here.' The woman answered and said to Him, 'I have no husband.' Jesus said to her, 'You have correctly said, "I have no husband"; for you have had five husbands, and the one whom you now have is not your husband; this which you have said is true.' The woman said to Him, 'Sir, I perceive that You are a prophet.'

For their first conversation, this is a pretty confrontational dialogue. Definitely not polite. Would you be so bold to ask someone such vulnerable details in order to lead them to a palace of revelation? The love of God compels us to meet people at the well and dig a well in their hearts. Digging a well is messy. We must dig past the worms and pull out the stones until we reach the precious resource below the surface. Jesus knew that this line of questioning would lead this woman to a place of transformation.

questions: Do you tend to live in the comfort zone or the conflict zone?
What would it take for you to step into uncomfortable conversations for the sake of someone's freedom?

lessons.title: A Little Bit of Splagna Goes a Long Way

content.text: At the end of Matthew 9 we find this interesting conversation between Jesus and His disciples. He commands them to earnestly pray (to plead with, to beg) Him to send out workers to gather the harvest. The Greek word used here for send out is {{ekballo|Greek for thrust out or send violently; the same word used when Jesus casts out demons}}. It is a term of violent force. It really means to thrust out. Ekballo gives us the imagery of someone being thrown through a door. Ekballo is the same word the Bible uses when Jesus 'casts out' demons. We know that the disciples are the workers and the crowds are the harvest. But what was it that caused Him to say these things?

This leads us to our next Greek word {{splagna|Greek for compassion; to be moved deeply in your inward parts}} that translates to compassion. It means to be moved in the inward parts. Splagna is the feeling you would get if you lost someone you loved dearly. It's the turning of your stomach, the agony of heartbreak and the love sick feeling that would cause us to reject a hot meal.

When Jesus saw the crowds, He had splagna for them. They were lost, harassed and without a protector. Without splagna the ekballo would never happen. Splagna is the driving force behind the violent action of sending. Every time Jesus took action it was because He was moved with splagna. Both times Jesus fed the hungry crowds; compassion. The two blind men who cried out to him; compassion. The widow and her dead son; compassion. Lazarus.

When Jesus saw her weeping, and the Jews who had come along with her also weeping, he was deeply moved in spirit and troubled. 'Where have you laid him?' he asked. 'Come and see, Lord,' they replied. Jesus wept.

content.prompt: When you see people who don't know Jesus, does your heart break? Does your stomach turn? Do you feel helpless or do you know how to take action?

content.text: Jesus called his twelve disciples to him and gave them authority to drive out impure spirits and to heal every disease and sickness.

Here we find our third Greek word {{exousia|Greek for authority; the power to act on behalf of one with great power}}. Exousia means authority. It's the authority to act on behalf of one with great power. But before the disciples could have access to this authority they had to experience the splagna (compassion) of Jesus which caused Him to ekballo (thrust forth).
When your heart breaks with Jesus you get sent out with the power that provides solutions. In this case the authority was to drive out demons and heal every disease and sickness.

In Christ you have a badge of authority (exousia) to use in any situation that doesn't look like heaven. Does your heart break for orphans? Ask Jesus to send you and he will provide the authority needed. Does your heart break for those with cancer? Ask to be ekballo'd and the authority will be given as well. You get the idea. Any area of life that does not line up with heaven is an area that God has authority over. But 95% of the time he waits for someone to ask to be sent.

content.prompt: Where is God sending you? What kind of authority do you need for where He will send you?
Ask God: 'Where do you want to send me? What authority are you giving me for that assignment?'

sessions.title: Training Day
sessions.synopsis: Time to put it all into practice. Learn practical strategies for surveying, door knocking, and everyday conversations that lead to eternal impact.
sessions.verse: Do not worry beforehand about what you are to say, but say whatever is given you at that time; for you are not the ones speaking, but it is the Holy Spirit.
warmUp: Today is the day we go out and put all of this into practice. How are you feeling about this session?

supportingScriptures.text: And Jesus said to him, 'Today salvation has come to this house, because he, too, is a son of Abraham. For the Son of Man has come to seek and to save that which was lost.'
How, then, can they call on the one they have not believed in? And how can they believe in the one of whom they have not heard? And how can they hear without someone preaching to them?
Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, and teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you.

content.text: This technique is not for everyone but it really works well at a mall, university campus or large outdoor gathering. We love to do this at Universities because students love to talk about themselves and we get to have cool conversations with them because of it. There are a variety of surveys you can use. We made our own on the topic of identity because it leads into an ARK bible study if they are open to it.
The looking glass self is a sociology theory that says 'You will become what the most important person in your life thinks about you.'

Three Questions:
1. Who is the most important person and what do they want you to become?
2. Are you becoming what they want for you?
3. What if God became the most important in your life? How would that change your life?

This survey reveals everything you need to know about this person. What kind of family do they come from? Who do they want to be? And what relationship do they have with God? From here, you can be led by the Holy Spirit to ask them specific questions that will help lead them into an encounter with God.

How do you think God sees you?
What if you could ask God what He sees when He looks at you?
Have you ever heard God speak to you?

You can share a testimony at this point. How does God speak to you? What has been your experience? Or maybe God is speaking to you about them. During this conversation, I would silently be asking 'God, when you look at this person what do you see?'. I might say 'As you were answering these questions I heard God say this... about you.'

questions: What context would work best for you to use the survey technique?
What questions would you add to make it your own?

content.text: Door knocking is fun when people open the door. Sometimes you find yourself doing more knocking than talking. Door knocking has gotten a bad rap from the Jehovah's Witnesses who use fear tactics to communicate the Bible. But that doesn't mean it's not an effective strategy for reaching people. I have had some amazing encounters with people right in their living room because I had the tenacity to go through their neighborhood and simply offer prayer.

content.title: Here's how I approach people at their door:

content.text:
Knock Knock (Who's there?)

'Hey, my name is Travis. I'm with a local church here in the area and we are just getting to know our neighbors. Is there anything you would like prayer for?'

(Noooo, Get off my porch!!!)

Very rarely do you get this response.

90% of the time you will get a 'No, I'm ok but thank you.' Or 'I'm not religious but thanks.' But often you will have someone open the door who has been asking for help. And you are the answer to their prayer. Maybe they are dealing with sickness and need healing. Maybe they haven't been to church in years but have been thinking about God. Maybe they are in a major life crisis and are starting to lose hope.

Will you be bold enough to knock on their door? I've even had God direct me to knock on specific doors. Holy Spirit knows each household that you approach. He will guide you and give you inside information if you are willing to ask. That's the key. We must ask, we must seek, we must knock. Will you get out of your comfort zone and do something dangerous today?
And Jesus said to him, 'Today salvation has come to this house, because he, too, is a son of Abraham. For the Son of Man has come to seek and to save that which was lost.'

content.prompt: What fears come up when you think about door knocking? What would it take to overcome them?

content.text: 1. Have fun. People are drawn to joy and get curious when someone has it. Smile. Jesus loves your teeth.
2. Be normal. If you are in a store, shop. Ask employees questions. Spark up random conversation. Give people compliments.
3. Have an opening question that will work to start a conversation. This will become more natural the more you do this.
4. Ask God to highlight people to you. This way you are not wandering aimlessly looking like a stalker in the aisles of the grocery store.
5. You can go alone but it's better with a partner. You can encourage and pray for one another throughout the journey.
6. Pray. Pray before, during and after. There is a spiritual realm and an enemy that hates when we share our faith. Stay prayed up.
1. Don't walk around in a group of 10 all wearing the same outreach t-shirt. People get nervous around Christian gangs.
2. Don't be a stalker. Stalkers get kicked out of malls, walmarts and off campus. Be normal. Go shopping. Talk to people as you go.
3. Don't forget people's names. Names are important and show people that you care about them.
4. Don't bring your signs and banners. Let Jesus in you be the sign and wonder people are looking for.
5. Don't approach people that may appear inappropriate. For example, if two grown men go around talking to teenage girls at the mall. This can be weird even if you have the best intentions.
6. Don't debate with people. If they believe differently, that's ok. The point is not to convince them but to win them. This may not be the only time you will run into them.

content.prompt: It's time. Go out and practice what you've learned.
content.action: Choose a strategy (survey, door knocking, or natural conversation), find a partner, pray together, and go share your faith. Come back with testimonies to share with the group.
